auth_notes: Most Rails APIs utilize either a simple API Key in the headers or a Bearer Token (JWT) via gems like Devise or Doorkeeper. If the portal uses session-based auth, CSRF tokens must be handled.
rate_limits: Dependent on server-side middleware like Rack::Attack; typically defaults to 60-100 requests per minute in production environments.
gotchas: Rails 'Wrap Parameters' can cause unexpected nesting in the JSON body sent to the API.
CSRF protection may interfere with POST/PUT requests if the API is not properly scoped to skip forgery protection.
ActiveRecord errors often return a 422 Unprocessable Entity status with a specific error object structure that needs parsing.
client_must_provide: Access to configure Webhook URLs in the portal admin
research_notes: Integration requires the n8n HTTP Request node. Authentication should be set up as a 'Header Auth' credential. For webhooks, the Rails application must be configured to send a POST request to the n8n Webhook URL, likely triggered by an 'after_commit' callback in the relevant Rails model.

effort_recommendation.rationale: Integration requires the n8n HTTP Request node. Authentication should be set up as a 'Header Auth' credential. For webhooks, the Rails application must be configured to send a POST request to the n8n Webhook URL, likely triggered by an 'after_commit' callback in the relevant Rails model.
